summary:
Claim: Surgical site infections in clean-contaminated wounds remain at 1–2% with proper prophylactic antibiotic use.  
Evidence: - Global health authorities (CDC/WHO) recommend and validate SSI rates of 1–2% for such wounds under optimal care. - Systematic reviews confirm that timely, appropriately dosed prophylactics effectively mitigate infection risks despite environmental contamination.  
Answer: A  

(487 tokens)

The claim is that surgical site infections (SSIs) in clean-contaminated wounds occur at a rate of 1–2% when prophylactic antibiotics are used appropriately. Key evidence includes endorsement by major health organizations such as the CDC and WHO, which cite 1–2% as the target SSI rate for clean-contaminated procedures under standard preventive measures; additionally, systematic reviews demonstrate that prompt and correct administration of prophylactic antibiotics consistently reduces actual infection rates within this range. The answer is therefore **A**. This reflects current clinical consensus based on both policy guidance and robust epidemiological data, even amid inherent increased microbial exposure in these wounds. Proper protocol adherence ensures infection control remains effective, validating the safety and efficacy of routine prophylaxis in this setting. (486 tokens)
